speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape five. It is May 31, 1995. This is an interview with Livia Bitton-Jackson. How did the director of the factory at Augsburg decide which Jewish women would do which of the jobs?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that's a wonderful question. We were lined up when we arrived. And this-- Herr, or Mr. Zerkuebbel was his name, I remember. Z-E-R-K-U-E-B-B-E-L, Zerkuebbel. He was a tall, red-headed man. He was the head of the factory. And it was his job to select according to intelligence. You know, the more intelligent people will do the more sensitive jobs, and the less intelligent, the more menial jobs.

speaker: interviewee
text: And how was intelligence decided? He had a ruler in his hand. First of all, all the people with blond hair, blue eyes, very white skin were put in one group. They were already the most intelligent group. That was the superior intelligence. Then the next group was people with brown hair but blue eyes. That was the next group-- light color eyes. That was next in intelligence quotient.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the third group consisted of people who had the brown hair, brown eyes, but were, you know, tallish. And then last came the people with very dark brown hair and dark brown eyes, and they were short. Now, for instance, this Dr. Tauber, the doctor-- she also came with us in that transport-- she's a short lady with brown hair and brown eyes. And she was in the last group, the least intelligent. And I was a 13-year-old girl who hadn't even started high school, but I was in the superior group. And then afterwards, he took us, only us. The others were assigned different jobs in the factory according to this grouping. But the superior group then was further selected. And he came with this ruler in his hand. And he measured the distance between the cheekbones and the eyes and the forehead and the width of the forehead. I don't know what it meant. But he kept measuring this way and that way and the length of the nose. And then I, again, made it. And I was in this superior group. And I was given this very responsible, very carefully supervised job. And I really wasn't up to it. It took time until I learned it, and I was always in a panic that I should make a mistake because if you make a mistake, you could be very badly penalized.

speaker: interviewee
text: So because of my physical appearance, I was deemed highly intelligent. And unfortunately, that gave me a very, very big burden to carry, burden of a very sophisticated job.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, we were up to the point where we were shipped from Augsburg to one of the subsidiary camps of Dachau. The understanding was that the allied front is approaching, and they are concentrating the inmates. We were brought to a camp called Muehldorf. In Muehldorf-- near Muehldorf, there was a cement factory. And most of the-- mostly men were there, in Muehldorf. We didn't meet any women when we arrived.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we heard that among the men is also my brother from people-- you know, again, very quickly, how do you find these things out? Because as soon as you arrive, the men on the other side of the fence-- you know, there was this barbed wire fence-- come to the fence, and they shout, where do you come from? And you shout the name of the town. And they-- you ask them, where do you come from? And then by the name of the town, they would ask, is so-and-so among you? And then we say, is so-and-so among you? Now, I heard that there are men from Somorja. And we said, is Mark Friedman, Markus-- my father-- is he there? No, he's not there. Who is there from Somorja? One person. His name is Bubi Friedman. So I knew my brother is there. This is how we found out that my brother is there. And then we told them, tell him to come to the fence because we want to meet him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in every camp, you knew that just before sunset, the guards would change. There was a changing of the guard. And one guard would walk away this way. And the next guard would, you know, march-- you know, the goose step-- they would march and come from the other. There was a gap when that particular part of the fence would be unguarded. And then you could come through the fence quickly, and the other person would come to the fence, and you would discover each other, would see each other.

speaker: interviewee
text: So people told my brother that we are here, my mother and myself. And we saw the men were skeletal. So that morning, when we got our piece of bread, my mother said, let's eat only one piece, and let's throw over the fence for him the other piece because the men were in worse shape than we were. So this is what we did. We approached the fence with the piece of bread in hand. When my brother came, my mother didn't recognize him. Neither did I. He was skeletal, a walking skeleton. He looked awful. And he was dragging one leg. He had tattered clothes. He was a young boy, by now approaching 18, and his face was fuzzy with fresh hair as he was grown. His face was badly bruised. His eyes were deep in their sockets. He was really a walking skeleton.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we knew the walking skeletons were called muselmann. I don't know why they were called that. And we knew that a muselmann had a very short time to live. They usually just dropped dead because they already lost their will to live. They lost most of their mental processes. So he was in the last stage. And as he was approaching and rattling-- making a rattling noise because he had a metal bowl tied around his waist, and his waist also was bony, and as it hit his waist, he was coming with this rattle. And we looked at him, and there was this unearthly looking creature. And my mother was looking. And he looked at us, and he slowly-- in Hungarian, he said, mommy, you can throw that piece of bread over the fence. The guide doesn't mind. Because the guide was coming, and he already knew that in this camp, the guides did not mind talking to each other. It was OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he thought my mother's holding the bread and not throwing it because she's hesitating, she's afraid from the guide. And my mother didn't want to throw it because she didn't believe it was him. And I said, mommy, it is him. That's Bubi. I know. I recognize his voice. And my mother says, no. That's not my son.

speaker: interviewee
text: But then again, he says, mommy, just throw the bread over the fence. So then she threw the bread over the fence, and he bent down to pick it up, and he fell because he couldn't do that much anymore. So he picked up the bread, and he walked away. And this is how we met every evening at the fence for the next three weeks. We were in there three weeks. During this time, every evening we spared one bread, and we gave it to him. And then at the end of the three weeks, we were evacuated. We were put on trains. We didn't see my brother anymore because the men were in a separate segment of the train. Now I know that between 25,000 to 30,000 people were on that train. They had this long, long, long train. And they just took us somewhere. We didn't know where. Tuesday morning, we were put on the train, crowded, without food or drink. Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday, Friday, the train just kept going and going and going. Friday noon, we stood in a place. The doors were opened from the outside. We saw prisoners in striped uniform. And they said in Yiddish-- I didn't know Yiddish very well, but we understood because we knew German. And they said, we are liberated. We are free. The war is over. Get out.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we got out of the train. And the train was on a very high embankment, and I saw a field of corn, and in the distance there was a village. And by then, the prisoners-- thousands of them-- filled the fields. And they were eating the leaves from the corn. You know how corn looks, has these long green leaves. And people were devouring it. And I said to my mother, let's go. Let's go and eat some leaves of the corn. We were ravenous. This is already-- how many days? Fourth day. So my mother said, no, I'm not moving. I'm waiting for Bubi because he's on this train, and we have to meet him. I'm not going to be separated from him. So we stayed at the tracks. Most people left. We were just walking alongside the train, looking in every wagon to see whether we see him. And then we saw him walking towards us, slowly, slowly. So together we sat down near the tracks, and we were waiting for something to happen. He wasn't able to walk down the embankment. So we were just sitting there. And suddenly, there was shooting. And there was shooting from all sides and they screamed, back into the wagons, in German-- [GERMAN].

speaker: interviewee
text: And we did not know what happened, but we understood that possibly in the village, the Germans found out that the Americans are not here yet, that the front has not reached yet. So quickly, they drove us back into the-- into the trains, but many, many people got killed. They were shooting randomly, screaming, quickly, quickly, back into the trains. I could see the green field covered with splotches of red. And as we were driven back into the train, dead bodies were lying all over. Many people were injured, and they managed to drag themselves back into the train. My mother said, I'm not going to be separated from Bubi. So she took off a piece of her dress. She tore off from her skirt and tied it around his-- and make believe that he's a woman. It made him a woman, a piece of [NON-ENGLISH] on his head, and says, he's a woman. And we take him into our train.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were driven back, quite a few wounded among us. And the doors locked. Nobody really cared whether men or women, so it was OK he was with us. And we were just driven further on. This was Friday.

speaker: interviewee
text: The next day at noon, again the train stood still. And this time we were told that the Red Cross is here, and we're going to get soup. This is the first, by the way, that we ever heard of the International Red Cross. They came. They were going to give us soup.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we propped up my brother. I said, prepare your bowls. And people lined up. And they could reach out the little window that we'll reach out, and this bowl full of soup was given to them, you know, a ladle. And they took it, and they quickly ate. It was delicious smell, warm soup. So I was holding my brother-- he couldn't stand-- while he was holding his bowl, and at that moment, shooting started. The most savage shooting you can imagine, machine gun fire from all over. My brother got hit in the head. The soup was full of blood. Blood was spurting from his forehead. One arm was hit. He fell backwards. I fell down with him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the machine gun fire penetrated the walls of the-- of the wagon and also the roof of the wagon. From all sides, bullets or machine gun fire was zigzagging. And everybody was falling on the ground. And I quickly took my metal bowl, put it on my head, because once I read that this is the way you survive. You put something on your head. And again-- I don't want to go into details. Once the attack was over, the, the Germans this time opened the train doors and said, quickly, get out. Another wave of attack is coming. And we didn't understand why they went. Quickly, they said, get into the forest. There was a forest nearby. And my brother could not make it to the forest. So we hid under the-- under the wagon, the three of us. And low flying planes were shooting at us. And I saw it was an American plane. The American Air Force was attacking us. There was a second attack. And then there was a third wave of attack again. These planes went away, and another one came. And again, they sprayed machine gun fire into the train, under the train. They even flew, flew low and sprayed the people among the trees who were-- there was a clearing there. And the plane flew low, and I could see as they sprayed machine gun fire at the people, and they were dying as they were hiding between the trees. And then when the third wave was over, we were ordered back again into the trains. We didn't understand what was going on. Many people were terribly wounded. And I'm describing it in my book. One of my friends' leg was torn off by the knee. And I grabbed the lower stump-- such an insane idea-- and I was holding it against her knee, and I said, Lilly, hold it. Hold it. I thought by holding it, it will grow back together. It was awful. One of my friends or I was shot at. Awful injuries. And the doors closed, and the train moved on again. We didn't know why the Americans attacked us, but we thought maybe because they thought it was a military train. But they saw the uniforms. They saw the prison uniforms, and they sprayed bullets, on them too as they were hiding, so I didn't-- we didn't know what was going on. By now, we are hungry and wounded and shivering from cold because the train was going into the mountains, the Bavarian mountains. We were heading for Austria. This is-- this is Saturday noon. All night the train goes. Many people die during this night. We continued all day Sunday. Monday morning, the doors open. The train stood still. And an American officer walked into the wagon and asked who we were. First, he spoke English, and nobody understood him. But we knew that it must be an American because had a different uniform than the German. And then he went out. And he brought in someone else. Another officer came in who must've spoken a little bit of Yiddish. So he, he must have been a Jewish officer who spoke Yiddish to us, and we barely understood him. But we understood that he's telling us that he's an American, and they are our liberators, and now we are free.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he asked us, are you men or women? And we said, we are women. And where do you come from? And we said, we, we are prisoners from Dachau. And then they opened the train doors. And they said, those who cannot walk, we're going to carry them out. There were many dead bodies and many badly injured.

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother was carried out. He was still alive. It turned out that he was only straved in the head. And as a matter of fact, I sent him the form that you gave me, so he's going to be interviewed as well. And we were-- he was carried out. And my mother and I, we crawled out of there. And it was a town called Seeshaupt. It was a small resort place on a beautiful lake in Bavaria. They, it seemed, had not known anything about what's going on.

speaker: interviewee
text: When we were carried out-- and we also laid down among the tracks-- the German citizens were there. The leading citizens of Seeshaupt were brought by the Americans to the train to see what the Germans had done. And, and this officer-- one of them spoke to them in German and said, look at what your government had done. This is what the Nazi policy had accomplished. Have you ever seen people in a state like that? And they were just shocked. And they kept saying, oh my, oh my. We didn't know. We didn't know. We had no idea. And one middle-aged woman came over to me and said, oh my. Did you have to work also? And I said, yes, we had to work also. Oh, that must have been very difficult for you to work. For older people, must have been very difficult. I said, what do you mean, for older people? She says, well, at your age, must have been hard not to eat and also work. And I said, why, how old do you think I am? She said, 60, 62, 61. And I looked at her. I said, I am 14 years old. And she almost fainted. She apologized. She said, sorry. Oh, sorry. How should I know? How should I know? She made the sign of the cross on herself. Jesus, Maria, she said. And she walked away. So you can imagine how we look.

speaker: interviewee
text: And now I just want to interject something at this point, that on April 30th of this year, the town of Seeshaupt decided to commemorate the event. They remember-- the younger people remember. The older people probably are not alive anymore who were there. Younger people remembered especially-- it seems that there was a 9-year-old boy who was the son of the mayor who was also brought to the train. And it seems that he was traumatized by what he saw. And they decided to do a commemoration and invite all those who survived the-- up to today and who had been on that train. So the letter reached me, and I did go. And at this commemoration, there was one event where the pictures that the Americans had took were displayed. And there was a younger German woman who spoke at this exhibit and said that she was a young girl. She was only 14 at the time. And she was taken to the train together with everybody. And as they approached the train station, her mother quickly put her hand on her eyes and said, you cannot look. I don't want you to see this. And they didn't let her look. And she said she didn't see what she saw.

speaker: interviewee
text: So at this point, I walked over to her, and-- this was taken on German television-- and I said to her, shake hands. I said, I was 14 at the time. And [SOBBING] I was part of that spectacle that you were not allowed to see because you were too young. So everybody was crying-- everybody, all the Germans. There were many German citizens there who were-- broke down and were crying at this scene. So you can imagine-- I had no idea how we looked, but it gave me an idea.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, the other thing that I wanted to mention and-- should I bring in now--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --the American attack on our train? You see, all along we were under the impression that the American Air Force attacked us because they saw that it was a military train. And also, the head of the train, the commandant, was captured in Seeshaupt and later interrogated by the Americans, he told that he had order. Why was he heading for Austria, they asked him. So he said, because he was given an order to take the train into a tunnel in the Austrian Alps and blow it up. But now I found out during this commemoration-- you see, the people of Seeshaupt were planning this for a long time. And they were doing research into the whole event because they really didn't know much about it. So based on declassified documents, they found out something very, very spectacular, very amazing. And I'm going to shorten this. You see, Hitler was building a Alpine Fortress, it was called. And people who study the history of World War II-- they know about it. Whoever I mention to and is knowledgeable knew that a fortress was being built in a very deep valley called Otztal in Austria, in the Austrian Alps, in the very high Alps. And there, Hitler was going to build a cement haven, like an enormous bunker, for his surviving army. And so when the Americans and the British were closing in on his armies, he was going to ship them to Otztal with all the remaining ammunition and continue the war from there. And the Allies knew about it. And they knew that that fortress was impenetrable. They wouldn't be able to destroy it, and the war would have continued for a long time after it actually ended. So it would have caused many more American casualties.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the plan was-- and the order came down from Eisenhower-- to find this train-- oh yes, so I must say that the reason we were evacuated from Muehldorf and two more camps nearby who-- the inmates all worked in a cement factory. Through these people who knew how to work with cement were being shipped to Otztal to complete the Alpine Fortress. This was like a last minute, desperate effort by Hitler to prolong the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so this is where our train was heading in Austria. These inmates were going to build and complete this fortress. And this is the reason why the Americans not only happened on our train. They were looking for us. Documents show that they found us at a point called Poing, P-O-I-N-G, where the train was attacked. And the order was, completely destroy the train, not only the train, but the people in it because they were considering that if the train is destroyed, maybe it will be shipped by trucks to complete the work. But the aim was to kill everyone. Now, to me, this was such a shocking news that I didn't want to believe it. And Dr. Westebe, Peter Westebe in Seeshaupt, who was doing some of the research, promised me to send me xeroxed documents, a xeroxed copy of all the documents in which he found this information. And if this is true, then this is a fantastic story of a last minute annihilation by our liberators. Once we were liberated in Seeshaupt-- by the way, I don't want to believe it until I see those documents. I haven't received them yet. I spoke to him last Wednesday. I phoned him. And he said that he's almost ready to send me the entire package with pictures and documents. Now, the liberation was a horrible affair because the army, the Americans, were still fighting. They were not ready to treat--

speaker: interviewer
text: What day were you actually liberated?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were actually liberated on April 30, 1945. We stayed in Seeshaupt for another week, during which the war ended. And many, many of the injured died because there was no medical treatment. Again, I-- in order to go into detail would take too long. But I understand that the young American soldiers were fighting a war. They didn't expect what they saw. And they did not-- were not certainly prepared for treating sick people or injured or give us food. So it was up to the Germans. And some Germans set up sort of cauldrons on the street, and they cooked potatoes, and they gave us food. But many people continued dying after liberation. From here, we were taken to a large military camp in Munich called Flakkaserne where we were about a month. And from here, people who were-- who, who decided not to return to their homes were then again shipped to other camps, refugee camps that were called DP camps, displaced persons camp. Those who decided to go back to their homes, which was called repatriation-- you had to sign up. My mother, my brother, and I-- we signed up for repatriation and we were put on-- because we wanted to go home and see who else returned. We were sure my father survived because he was still young and athletic. And so we were-- we were put on trucks, American vehicles, and driven to Czechoslovakia one month after Seeshaupt. And at that time, they weighed us. I was already 53 kilo, and my brother was still only 45 kilo. And he was a very-- he's a very tall man. He was 6' 1' or whatever. Now, I don't know how much he's-- 53 kilos.
